exercises.title: Entropy of a distribution
exercises.prompt: Compute the Shannon entropy (in bits) of a fair coin and a biased coin [0.9, 0.1]. Explain why the fair coin has higher entropy.
exercises.solution:
```python
import math
def entropy(p): return -sum(x*math.log2(x) for x in p if x > 0)
print(entropy([0.5, 0.5]))   # 1.0 bit
print(round(entropy([0.9, 0.1]), 3))   # 0.469 bits
```

The fair coin has maximum entropy (1 bit) because it's maximally unpredictable — every outcome equally surprising. The biased coin is mostly predictable (usually heads), so it carries less information per flip. Entropy is the average surprise, and it's the floor on how few bits you can compress the outcomes into.

exercises.title: KL divergence
exercises.prompt: Compute KL(P‖Q) for P=[0.5,0.5] and Q=[0.9,0.1], then compute KL(Q‖P), and show they differ. Explain what KL measures and why the asymmetry matters in ML.
exercises.hint: KL(P‖Q) = Σ p·log2(p/q). It's the extra bits from coding P with a code built for Q.
exercises.solution:
```python
import math
def kl(p, q): return sum(pi*math.log2(pi/qi) for pi, qi in zip(p, q) if pi > 0)
print(round(kl([0.5,0.5], [0.9,0.1]), 3))   # 0.737
print(round(kl([0.9,0.1], [0.5,0.5]), 3))   # 0.531
```

KL(P‖Q) is the *extra* bits you pay encoding samples from P using a code optimized for Q — zero only when P=Q. It's **asymmetric**: KL(P‖Q) ≠ KL(Q‖P). The direction matters in practice — VAEs minimize KL(q‖p) (mode-seeking, q avoids regions where p is small), while forward KL (mode-covering) behaves differently. Picking the wrong direction changes what your model learns to do.

exercises.title: Cross-entropy = entropy + KL
exercises.prompt: Show numerically that cross-entropy H(P,Q) = H(P) + KL(P‖Q), and explain why this identity means minimizing cross-entropy loss is equivalent to minimizing KL divergence to the true labels.
exercises.hint: H(P,Q) = −Σ p·log2(q). Compute all three quantities and check the identity.
